summary: This PR adds support for generator-based WSGI applications by introducing a `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` helper and updating the dispatch logic. While the core approach is reasonable, there are correctness concerns around WSGI spec compliance (specifically `close()` handling), edge cases with zero-item generators, and a regression in test coverage where the list-returning WSGI app path is no longer exercised. (1 finding(s) below the 0.4 confidence threshold hidden.)

findings.title: AssertionError when generator WSGI app yields zero items (start_response never called)
findings.detail:
When `application_factory` is given an empty iterable (e.g. `output=[]`), the generator body never executes, so `start_response` is never called and `seen_status` remains `None`. The `assert seen_status is not None` on line 104 will then fire as an `AssertionError`. The existing test `test_wsgi_generator_empty` uses `[b"", b"", b"", b""]` (4 items), which causes the generator body to run and call `start_response` before its first yield — so the bug is not caught by that test. Passing `output=[]` would reproduce it.

More broadly, the assumption that `start_response` is called before the first `yield` holds for `application_factory` but is not guaranteed for arbitrary WSGI generators — a non-conforming (but real-world) generator could call `start_response` after the first yield, leaving `seen_status` still `None` at assertion time. The assertion should be replaced with a proper error rather than a bare `assert`, or the code should handle the case where `start_response` was never called gracefully.

findings.title: _skip_leading_empty_chunks discards close() when all chunks are empty, violating PEP 3333
findings.detail:
PEP 3333 requires that if the iterable returned by a WSGI app has a `close()` method, the server must call it after the response is complete. When `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` exhausts the entire iterator without finding a non-empty chunk, it returns a plain `[]` — dropping any reference to the original generator. This means that if the app's generator has cleanup code in a `finally` block or an explicit `close()` method, it will never be invoked, causing resource leaks.

The correct fix is to retain a reference to the original iterator (e.g. via a wrapper or by stashing it) and ensure `close()` is called on it when the response is done, even in the all-empty case.

findings.title: application_factory departs from the existing convention of plain top-level WSGI app functions in tests
findings.detail: All other WSGI app fixtures in the test file (`echo_body`, `raise_exc`, `echo_body_with_response_stream`) are defined as plain top-level functions, which makes them easy to read and understand. Replacing the original `hello_world` function with an `application_factory` closure factory is a stylistic departure. Consider keeping a plain `hello_world` function for the basic `test_wsgi` test and adding a separate `hello_world_generator` function (or similar) for the new generator-specific tests, to maintain consistency.

findings.title: Refactoring removed list-returning WSGI app test coverage; _skip_leading_empty_chunks fast path only indirectly tested
findings.detail:
The original `test_wsgi` test covered a plain list-returning WSGI app (`return [output]`). After refactoring to use `application_factory` (a generator), the list-return code path through `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` is no longer directly tested. `echo_body` and `echo_body_with_response_stream` still exist but do not exercise leading-empty-bytes scenarios with plain lists.

Additionally, the fast path of `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` (first chunk is non-empty, immediately returns `chain(...)`) is only exercised indirectly via a single-element generator in `test_wsgi`. There is no isolated or direct test for the helper with a plain list input containing leading empty bytes. Consider adding at least one test with a plain list WSGI response to guard against regressions.

findings.title: Missing return type annotation on _skip_leading_empty_chunks
findings.detail: The rest of the file annotates function return types consistently (e.g. `send` returns `Response`, `start_response` returns `None`). `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` has a parameter annotation (`typing.Iterable`) but no `-> typing.Iterable` return type annotation. Adding it would match the surrounding style.

findings.title: Slightly imprecise wording in inline comment near assertion
findings.detail: The comment wording 'shouldn't be used until the first non-empty chunk has been served' uses 'served' where 'yielded' or 'received' would be more technically accurate and consistent with the WSGI/iterator terminology used elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: Local variable name output in tests shadows the same name used inside application_factory closure
findings.detail: Both `test_wsgi_generator` and `test_wsgi_generator_empty` use `output` as a local variable name, matching the parameter name inside `application_factory`'s closure. While not a bug, using a name like `chunks` at the test level would visually distinguish test-level data from the app-internal variable, improving readability.

findings.title: seen_status is asserted non-None before start_response is called when all chunks are empty
findings.detail:
When the WSGI app is a generator and all chunks are empty, `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` exhausts the iterator without ever calling `start_response` (because `start_response` is only called when the generator body runs, which happens lazily during iteration). The assertion `assert seen_status is not None` on line 104 will then fire as an AssertionError even though the app technically completed successfully.

More specifically: `application_factory` creates a generator function. When `self.app(environ, start_response)` is called, the generator object is returned but **no code in its body runs yet** — including the `start_response(status, response_headers)` call. `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` then drives the generator forward; if all chunks are empty it returns `[]` and the loop ends. At that point `seen_status` is still `None` (or it may have been set — this depends on the exact interleaving). 

Actually, tracing more carefully: the generator body runs `start_response` *before* the first `yield`, so `start_response` IS called during the first `next()` call inside `_skip_leading_empty_chunks`. But only if the generator yields at least one item. If the generator yields zero items (empty iterable passed to `application_factory`), `start_response` is never called and `seen_status` remains `None`, causing the assertion to fail. The `test_wsgi_generator_empty` test passes `[b"", b"", b"", b""]` (4 items), which is enough to trigger the `start_response` call before yielding the first item, so that specific test passes. However, if `output=[]` were passed, the bug would manifest.

Additionally, even in the normal generator case, the real correctness concern is the ordering: the fix calls `_skip_leading_empty_chunks(result)` and then immediately asserts `seen_status is not None`. But `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` returns a (possibly lazy) iterable — it doesn't exhaust the generator, it only advances it to the first non-empty chunk. The assertions at lines 104-107 check `seen_status` *after* `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` returns, which is correct only because the generator runs `start_response` before its first yield. This is true for `application_factory` but is not guaranteed for all WSGI generators — if a generator called `start_response` only after the first yield (which is unusual but possible), `seen_status` would still be `None` here.

findings.title: _skip_leading_empty_chunks returns a plain list [] instead of an iterator when all chunks are empty, discarding close() support
findings.detail: The WSGI spec (PEP 3333) requires that if the iterable returned by the app has a `close()` method, the server must call it after the response is finished. When `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` exhausts all items without finding a non-empty one, it returns `[]` — a plain empty list — rather than an object that preserves the original generator's `close()` method. This means that if the app's generator has cleanup code in a `finally` block (or an explicit `close()` method), it will never be called. The correct approach would be to keep a reference to the original iterator and, on exhaustion, return an empty iterable that still delegates `close()` to the original.

findings.title: Return type annotation missing on helper function
findings.detail: The existing code in this file consistently annotates functions (e.g. `send` returns `Response`, `start_response` returns `None`). `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` has a parameter annotation (`typing.Iterable`) but no return type annotation. Adding `-> typing.Iterable` would match the surrounding style.

findings.title: Inline comment style slightly inconsistent with existing comments
findings.detail: The rest of the file uses no inline block comments near `assert` statements or result assignments. The new three-line comment block at lines 100-102 is reasonable, but the wording 'shouldn't be used until the first non-empty chunk has been served' is slightly imprecise ('served' could be 'yielded' or 'received'). Minor wording nit to match the more technical phrasing used elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: `application_factory` replaces the original standalone `hello_world` function with a more complex factory, making simple tests harder to read
findings.detail: The existing test style (visible in `echo_body`, `raise_exc`, `echo_body_with_response_stream`) defines each WSGI app as a plain top-level function. Introducing a factory that returns a closure is a departure from that convention. The original `hello_world` function (or a similarly named plain function) could be kept for `test_wsgi`, and a separate generator app function (e.g. `hello_world_generator`) could be added just for the new generator tests, keeping consistency with the surrounding style.

findings.title: Local variable `output` in test could shadow outer scope if tests are extended
findings.detail: Both `test_wsgi_generator` (line 99) and `test_wsgi_generator_empty` (line 107) use `output` as a local variable name, which matches the convention in the WSGI app functions (`output = ...`). This is fine in isolation, but the name `output` is also used inside `application_factory`'s closure as a parameter. This is a very minor readability nit — something like `chunks` would distinguish the test-level data from the app-level variable name used inside the factory.

findings.title: No test for _skip_leading_empty_chunks with a non-generator (list) WSGI response
findings.detail: The existing tests use application_factory which always yields (generator). The original list-returning WSGI app path (e.g. `return [output]`) now also goes through _skip_leading_empty_chunks. There is no test that passes a plain list with leading empty bytes to _skip_leading_empty_chunks, though this is lower risk since lists already work with iter(). The test_wsgi test was refactored to use the generator factory, losing the direct coverage of the list-return path going through the new helper.

findings.title: No test for _skip_leading_empty_chunks where the first chunk is non-empty (fast path)
findings.detail: The new helper _skip_leading_empty_chunks has two branches: (1) a non-empty chunk is encountered immediately (returns chain on first iteration), and (2) all chunks are empty (returns []). The test_wsgi test now uses application_factory with [b"Hello, World!"] which exercises the fast path through a generator, but there is no isolated unit test for _skip_leading_empty_chunks itself. More importantly, test_wsgi_generator covers leading empties followed by content, and test_wsgi_generator_empty covers all-empty. The first-chunk-non-empty branch is only tested via test_wsgi with a single-element list through the generator factory, which is a thin path. Confidence is moderate since it does exercise the branch indirectly.

findings.title: No test covering _skip_leading_empty_chunks with a mix of empty and non-empty chunks where remaining body items are also consumed correctly
findings.detail: The test_wsgi_generator test verifies [b"", b"", b"Some content", b" and more content"] produces the correct concatenated text. This is good. However, there is no test verifying that the remaining items after the first non-empty chunk are fully consumed (i.e., that itertools.chain correctly threads the rest of the iterator through). The current test does assert response.text == "Some content and more content" which implicitly covers this, so this is low risk, but it is done through a high-level response assertion rather than chunk-level verification.

findings.title: test_wsgi no longer tests the original list-returning WSGI app
findings.detail: The original test_wsgi tested a plain function returning a list ([output]). After refactoring, all tests now use application_factory which yields chunks via a generator. The list-return path (which is a valid and common WSGI pattern) is no longer covered by any test. echo_body and echo_body_with_response_stream still test list and generator returns, but they always return non-empty content. The _skip_leading_empty_chunks function operating on a plain list with leading empty bytes is untested.
